aliases: 泡菜炒饭 (Pào Cài Chǎo Fàn)

chinese: 泡菜炒饭 (Pào Cài Chǎo Fàn)

food_subgroup: Stir-fried rice dish with kimchi, pork or Spam, egg, gochujang, and sesame oil; served as one plate (~350g)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Kimchi Fried Rice; FOODON_03315393 (fried rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CCG (fried rice dish) used as closest parent; no specific Kimchi Fried Rice entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Kimchi Fried Rice or fried rice dish category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Kimchi Fried Rice weighs approximately 350g and provides roughly 550 kcal, 16g protein, 16g fat, and 72g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fried Rice (SR Legacy) combined with Kimchi composition (SR Legacy item 11548)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by combining USDA SR Legacy fried rice data with USDA SR Legacy kimchi composition (item 11548) and standard pork/egg/gochujang/sesame oil component data from USDA FoodData Central. No single USDA entry exists for Kimchi Fried Rice; values represent a weighted composite for a standard 350g plate preparation. Per-100g values calculated from the composite plate estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Fried Rice — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Am J Clin Nutr
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kimchi Fried Rice. GI of 70 is estimated from fried white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) with a marginal downward adjustment to ~70 reflecting: (1) partial starch retrogradation from high-heat stir-frying, (2) trace amylase inhibition from kimchi lactic acid, and (3) protein and fat co-ingestion from egg and pork moderating gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 72) / 100 = 50.4. Carbohydrate per 350g serving = 20.6 × 3.5 = 72.1g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1,200mg per 350g plate) from multiple compounding sources: kimchi fermentation (~600mg), soy sauce (~200mg), gochujang (~200mg), and Spam if used (~350mg additional).
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request pork belly substitution for Spam (saves ~350–400mg sodium per plate), limit gochujang quantity, and be aware that even the pork belly version delivers ~850mg sodium — approaching the upper limit for a single meal in a sodium-restricted diet; the Spam version at ~1,550mg sodium per plate is clinically unacceptable for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (GL ~50.4 per plate) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate; GI ~70 (high) with only marginal reduction from stir-frying retrogradation and kimchi lactic acid.
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should limit portion size to half a plate, practise meal sequencing by eating egg and protein components first to blunt postprandial glucose rise, avoid adding extra gochujang at the table (adds ~10–12g sugar per plate), and consider substituting 50% of white rice with cauliflower rice at home to reduce GL by approximately half while preserving flavour.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium (~1,200mg per plate), moderate saturated fat (~4.2g per plate from pork/Spam/egg), and high glycemic load (GL ~50.4) promoting postprandial triglyceride elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: CVD patients should avoid the Spam variant entirely due to its processed-meat sodium and saturated fat load, substitute pork belly in small quantities or use tofu as the protein component, and limit consumption to occasional meals rather than regular dietary inclusion given the compounding sodium, saturated fat, and high-GL profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1,200mg) is relevant for RA patients on corticosteroids; high GL (50.4) may promote postprandial inflammation; cooking destroys probiotic Lactobacillus in kimchi, eliminating any anti-inflammatory probiotic benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on corticosteroids should be particularly cautious of the high sodium content; sesame oil provides modest ALA omega-3 precursor and the egg contributes vitamin D and choline, but these benefits do not offset the high sodium and GL burden in a regular dietary context.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Fried Rice (김치볶음밥, Kimchi Bokkeumbap) is a quintessential Korean comfort dish made by stir-frying cooked white rice with fermented kimchi, pork belly or Spam, egg, gochujang (Korean chilli paste), soy sauce, and sesame oil in a very hot wok. The dish is Korea's most common weekday home-cooked meal — a practical and flavourful use of leftover rice and aged kimchi. Per standard plate (~350g): approximately 550 kcal, 16g protein, 16g fat, 72g carbohydrate, GI ~70 (high), GL ~50.4 (very high), and ~1,200mg sodium. The texture is slightly chewy and oily with a smoky wok-char aroma; the flavour is sour-spicy-savoury from the fermented kimchi and gochujang. A critical clinical point: the high-heat cooking process destroys all probiotic Lactobacillus cultures in the kimchi — kimchi fried rice must NOT be counselled as a probiotic food despite being made with fermented kimchi. The dish retains fermented flavour and lactic acid from kimchi but not live bacteria. The Spam variant adds ~350mg additional sodium per plate versus the pork belly variant. A practical GL-reduction strategy for home cooking: substitute 50% of white rice with cauliflower rice to approximately halve the GL while maintaining the characteristic kimchi fried rice flavour profile. In Singapore, kimchi fried rice is available at Korean restaurants and Korean-themed hawker stalls, often topped with a fried sunny-side-up egg.
common_user_queries: Kimchi fried rice with Spam sodium

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a USDA FoodData Central composite of fried rice (SR Legacy) and kimchi (SR Legacy item 11548) with standard component data for pork, egg, gochujang, and sesame oil. No single approved-source entry exists for Kimchi Fried Rice as a complete dish. GI is an analogous estimate from fried white rice data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Values are internally consistent and plausible but represent a weighted estimate rather than a direct laboratory measurement of the composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite Kimchi Fried Rice dish (white rice + kimchi + pork/Spam + egg + sesame oil + gochujang) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-14; individual component MUFA/PUFA data could be summed but would introduce compounding estimation error beyond the acceptable threshold for this field. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite cooked dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central component data for the standard preparation ingredients. Cooking process note: high-heat stir-frying destroys all probiotic Lactobacillus cultures in kimchi; this dish should not be counselled as a probiotic food. Spam variant adds approximately 350mg additional sodium per plate versus pork belly variant. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from fried white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Kimchi Fried Rice exists in approved sources. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315393 and FoodEx2 A0CCG used as closest-parent matches; no exact codes exist for Kimchi Fried Rice.
